skill.name: codebase-design
skill.description: Shared vocabulary for designing deep modules. Use when the user wants to design or improve a module's interface, find deepening opportunities, decide where a seam goes, make code more testable or AI-navigable, or when another skill needs the deep-module vocabulary.
skill.summary: Design deeper code modules with smaller interfaces, cleaner seams, and more focused tests.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported weak cryptography, shell backtick execution, and system reconnaissance patterns. AI review found these are false positives in Markdown documentation, code fences, filenames, and architecture prose, with no executable script, network behavior, secret access, or prompt injection evidence.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts occur in Markdown documentation, filenames, headings, and architecture terms. No evidence found of cryptographic APIs, cipher selection, hashing code, key material, or encryption logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files contain prose guidance and TypeScript examples, not cryptographic implementation. The matched lines do not perform encryption, hashing, signing, or key handling.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Treated as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick alerts align with Markdown fenced code blocks and documentation examples. No executable Ruby, shell script, command invocation, subprocess API, or user-controlled command path is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched ranges are Markdown diagrams and TypeScript snippets. There is no shell command syntax intended for execution by the skill.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern Match
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alert appears on a TypeScript example about returning results instead of side effects. No evidence found of host inspection, environment discovery, process listing, or filesystem enumeration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 90 is inside a short TypeScript example for testability. It does not reference system state, commands, files, environment variables, or network resources.

content.value_statement: Large codebases often grow shallow modules with broad interfaces and scattered behavior. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a shared vocabulary for designing deeper modules and cleaner tests.
actual_capabilities: Defines module, interface, seam, adapter, depth, leverage, and locality terms.
Helps assess whether modules are deep or shallow.
Guides interface design with fewer methods and simpler parameters.
Classifies dependencies before refactoring across a seam.
Recommends testing through the module interface instead of internals.
Supports parallel exploration of alternative interface designs.
limitations: It provides design guidance, not automated code changes.
It requires enough code context to judge module depth accurately.
It does not replace domain knowledge from maintainers.
It may need adaptation for strict framework or platform conventions.

use_cases.title: Refactor a shallow module
use_cases.description: Identify pass-through code and reshape it into a deeper module with a smaller caller surface.

use_cases.title: Plan testable interfaces
use_cases.description: Move tests toward stable behavior at the module interface instead of fragile implementation details.

use_cases.title: Compare architecture options
use_cases.description: Generate several interface designs and compare their depth, locality, dependency handling, and trade-offs.

prompt_templates.title: Find shallow modules
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small area
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this module using the codebase-design vocabulary. Tell me whether it is deep or shallow, and explain the caller burden.

prompt_templates.title: Improve one interface
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a smaller interface for this module. Keep the same behavior, hide more implementation detail, and describe the new seam.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a deepening refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Classify this module's dependencies, choose the right seam strategy, and propose a staged plan for deepening it safely.

prompt_templates.title: Compare design alternatives
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Design It Twice process. Produce three different interfaces, compare depth and locality, then recommend the strongest design.

output_examples.input: Review a payment orchestration module with many thin wrappers.
output_examples.output: The response identifies shallow wrappers, proposes one deeper payment workflow interface, and explains which dependencies should become adapters.

output_examples.input: Design tests for a refactored reporting module.
output_examples.output: The response recommends tests at the reporting interface, removes tests tied to internals, and lists observable outcomes to assert.

output_examples.input: Compare three possible interfaces for a search indexing module.
output_examples.output: The response contrasts each design by caller effort, hidden complexity, seam placement, dependency strategy, and long-term maintainability.

best_practices: Provide concrete files, call sites, and tests before asking for design guidance.
Ask for dependency classification before choosing a seam.
Use the same module, interface, seam, and adapter terms throughout the discussion.
anti_patterns: Do not add seams when only one adapter exists.
Do not expose internal seams just to make tests easier.
Do not judge module depth by implementation line count alone.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps design modules with smaller interfaces, more hidden behavior, and cleaner tests across stable seams.

faq.question: Does it edit code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture vocabulary and design guidance that an assistant or developer can apply.

faq.question: What is a deep module?
faq.answer: A deep module gives callers substantial behavior through a small interface with clear rules and error modes.

faq.question: How does it improve testing?
faq.answer: It encourages tests through the public module interface, so tests describe behavior instead of private implementation details.

faq.question: When should I use Design It Twice?
faq.answer: Use it when an important interface decision needs several different designs before choosing one direction.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The reviewed files are documentation, and static security alerts were false positives.
